name: Lets-Plot (ggplot2-Compatible Python Visualization)

performance.notes: Kotlin Multiplatform rendering; faster than plotnine for large datasets

agent_readiness.notes: ggplot2-faithful Grammar of Graphics for Python. from lets_plot import *. LetsPlot.setup_html(). p = ggplot(df, aes(x='x', y='y', color='group')) + geom_point() + geom_smooth() + scale_color_brewer(type='qual'). ggsave(p, 'plot.png'). Geoms: geom_bar, geom_line, geom_histogram, geom_boxplot, geom_violin, geom_density, geom_heatmap, geom_contour. Facets: facet_grid(), facet_wrap(). Scales: scale_x_log10(), scale_fill_manual(). Themes: theme_minimal(), theme_bw(). Stats: stat_bin(), stat_smooth(). Coordinate: coord_polar(). Annotations: geom_text(), geom_label(). Accurate ggplot2 semantics. Interactivity: plotly-like tooltips.

gotchas: Kotlin backend — heavier install than plotnine
Not 100% API-compatible with ggplot2/plotnine — some differences exist
Lesser community than R's ggplot2 or Python's matplotlib/seaborn
best_when: ggplot2 Grammar of Graphics faithful implementation in Python
When plotnine is too slow for larger datasets
R ggplot2 users migrating to Python who want familiar API
Interactive HTML plots with ggplot2 aesthetic quality
avoid_when: When plotnine's exact R compatibility is required
Simple quick plots (use matplotlib or seaborn)
summary: Lets-Plot implements ggplot2 Grammar of Graphics faithfully in Python. ggplot(df, aes(x='x', y='y')) + geom_point() + facet_wrap(). geom_bar, geom_line, geom_histogram, geom_boxplot. scale_color_brewer(). theme_minimal(). ggsave(). Interactive HTML tooltips. MIT by JetBrains. Faster than plotnine for large data. setup_html() required. R ggplot2 users migrate easily. Not 100% plotnine-compatible.
